title_en: Introduction: The Inner Dimensions of Prayer
content_en:
The Inner Dimensions of Prayer — known in Arabic as Asrar al-Salah — is one of the most beloved and widely studied works on the spiritual reality of the daily prayer by Ibn al-Qayyim al-Jawziyyah (d. 751 AH). While jurisprudential works on prayer deal with the external conditions, pillars, and rulings, Ibn al-Qayyim in this book penetrates to the internal — the spiritual states, the presence of heart, the meanings, and the profound inner journey that the prayer is designed to facilitate.

The prayer is the second pillar of Islam and the most recurring act of worship in a Muslim's life — five times daily at minimum. The Prophet ﷺ said: "The first matter that the servant will be judged on the Day of Judgment is the prayer; if it is good, all his deeds are good, and if it is corrupt, all his deeds are corrupt." (Al-Tabarani). Yet many Muslims acknowledge that they struggle to attain the presence of heart (khushu') that makes the prayer what it is meant to be — a genuine encounter with Allah, a source of tranquility and strength, a preventive against evil.

Ibn al-Qayyim explains that the prayer has an outer form and an inner soul, and that performing the form without the soul is like a body without a spirit. He explores the meanings of each element of the prayer: what it means to say Allahu Akbar (Allah is the Greatest), what the Fatiha is really saying when we recite it, what bowing and prostration mean for the soul's relationship with its Creator, what the final salaam represents. He also discusses the spiritual ailments that prevent khushu' — scattered thoughts, attachment to this world, heedlessness — and the remedies prescribed by the Quran, Sunnah, and the wisdom of the righteous predecessors.

This book transforms the prayer from a recurring obligation into a living encounter, and is essential reading for any Muslim seeking to revive the spirit of their worship.
